/lessons/gender-nouns-1: In Spanish, all nouns have a grammatical gender: they are either masculine or feminine.
While there are exceptions, the most common way to identify the gender of a noun is by its ending:
Always learn the noun with its article (el or la). This is the most reliable way to remember its gender.
/lessons/gender-nouns-2: Beyond -o and -a, there are other common endings that can help you determine a noun's gender.
Nouns ending in these suffixes are almost always feminine:
Nouns of Greek origin ending in
are usually masculine, even though they end in -a.

/lessons/articles: Spanish articles must agree in gender and number with the nouns they modify.

/lessons/regular-verbs-1: In Spanish, verbs are categorized by their endings: -ar, -er, or -ir. This lesson focuses on the most common group: -ar verbs.
To conjugate a regular -ar verb in the present tense, remove the -ar to find the stem, then add the following endings:
/lessons/regular-verbs-2: Regular -er verbs follow a pattern similar to -ar verbs, but using 'e' instead of 'a' in the endings.
To conjugate a regular -er verb in the present tense, remove the -er to find the stem, then add the following endings:
/lessons/regular-verbs-3: Regular -ir verbs are almost identical to -er verbs in their endings, with exceptions in the 'nosotros' and 'vosotros' forms.

/lessons/adjectives-1: In Spanish, adjectives must agree in both gender and number with the nouns they describe.
Adjectives follow the same rules as nouns for making plural forms.
or a consonant don't change for gender, only for number.
el libro grande / la casa grande
/lessons/adjectives-2: In Spanish, the position of an adjective is just as important as its form.
Most adjectives follow the noun they modify, which is the opposite of English.
English: red car → Spanish: car red
Adjectives that indicate quantity or order often precede the noun.
In poetry or literature, adjectives may precede the noun to add emphasis or a descriptive quality, but in daily speech, they almost always follow the noun.
/lessons/ser-estar-1: In English, the verb 'to be' is always the same. In Spanish, there are two distinct verbs:
/lessons/ser-estar-2: Ser vs Estar II (Uses of Ser)
is used to describe permanent or essential qualities.
/lessons/ser-estar-3: Ser vs Estar III (Uses of Estar)
is used to describe temporary states, moods, and locations.
/lessons/ser-estar-4: can change the meaning of an adjective.
is for WHAT something is, and
is for HOW something is at the moment.

/lessons/interrogatives: Spanish uses unique punctuation and specific question words to ask questions.
In Spanish, questions always start with an inverted question mark (
) and end with a standard one (
Notice that all question words in Spanish have an accent mark when used in a question.
/lessons/numbers-1: Counting is a basic skill in any language. Let's start with the first ten numbers in Spanish.
/lessons/numbers-2: As you go higher, Spanish numbers follow a pattern, especially after 20.
For numbers between tens, use 'y' (and) for 31 and above:
/lessons/telling-time: To tell time in Spanish, we use the verb
followed by the feminine article to agree with 'las horas'.
/lessons/dates: Talking about dates in Spanish follows a very specific formula.
While we use regular numbers for most dates, for the first day of the month, we use
Months in Spanish are not capitalized unless they start a sentence.
enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre
/lessons/possessives-1: Possessive adjectives indicate ownership or relationship. In Spanish, they must agree in number with the noun they modify.
/lessons/possessives-2: For 'our' and 'your' (plural), the possessive adjectives also agree in gender.
/lessons/irregular-verbs-yo: Some common Spanish verbs are regular in all forms except for the first-person singular (yo).
/lessons/saber-conocer: Both verbs mean 'to know,' but they are used in completely different contexts.
Used for facts, information, and skills (how to do something).
Used for people, places, and things (being familiar with).
Both are irregular in the yo form:
/lessons/personal-a: When the direct object of a verb is a person, Spanish requires a special preposition: the personal
It doesn't translate to English, but it must be there if the object is human or a pet.
/lessons/gustar: doesn't mean 'to like' in the way English does. It literally means 'to be pleasing to.'
The thing being 'liked' is actually the subject of the sentence. Therefore, we mostly use only two forms:
I like coffee (Coffee is pleasing to me).
I like books (Books are pleasing to me).
You must use a pronoun with gustar to show who is pleased:
me, te, le, nos, os, les
/lessons/stem-changers-e-ie: Stem-changing verbs change their spelling in the root of the word for all forms except nosotros and vosotros.
These are often called 'boot verbs' because if you circle the changing forms on a standard grid, it looks like a boot!
/lessons/stem-changers-o-ue: Just like e->ie verbs, these verbs change the 'o' in the stem to 'ue' for all forms except nosotros/vosotros.
/lessons/stem-changers-e-i: A smaller group of -ir verbs change the 'e' in the stem to a simple 'i'.
/lessons/affinity-words: Spanish uses pairs of words to affirm or deny the existence of people or things.
In English, double negatives are avoided. In Spanish, they are required!
I don't see anyone (I don't see no one).
/lessons/por-para-1: In English, both 'por' and 'para' are often translated as 'for.' However, in Spanish, they have distinct uses that are never interchangeable.
as expressing movement through a space, duration of time, or cause/reason.
(through, along, by, or in the area of)
(because of, on account of, on behalf of)
/lessons/por-para-2: While 'por' is about the 'through' or the 'why,'
is about the 'for' in terms of destination, goal, or deadline.
as expressing a destination, a purpose, or a point in the future.
I study in order to learn (Purpose).
/lessons/adverbs: Adverbs describe how, when, or where an action occurs. Many Spanish adverbs are formed from adjectives.
To form an adverb from an adjective, use the feminine singular form and add
Adverbs that modify verbs usually come
the verb. Adverbs that modify adjectives or other adverbs come
/lessons/comparisons-inequality: In Spanish, we use specific formulas to compare things that are not equal (more than or less than).
Juan es más alto que Pedro.
I have less money than you.
Some adjectives have their own comparative forms:
/lessons/comparisons-equality: To compare things that are equal (as... as), Spanish uses different formulas depending on whether you are using adjectives or nouns.
Ella es tan inteligente como él.
She is as intelligent as him.
Notice that 'tanto' must agree in gender and number with the noun.
I have as many books as you.
There is as much people (as many people) as yesterday.
/lessons/superlatives: Superlatives are used to describe something that has the highest or lowest degree of a quality (the most, the least).
Juan es el más alto de la clase.
Juan is the tallest in the class.
Es la película menos interesante de todas.
It is the least interesting movie of all.
To say something is 'extremely' or 'very' [adjective], you can add the suffix
/lessons/pronouns-prep-objects: When a pronoun follows a preposition (like for, with, about), Spanish uses specific forms that are mostly identical to subject pronouns.
/lessons/direct-objects-1: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the action of a verb, helping to avoid repetition.
In a simple sentence with one conjugated verb, the direct object pronoun must come
/lessons/direct-objects-2: When a sentence contains two verbs (like 'I want to see it'), you have a choice of where to place the pronoun.
Option 2: Attached to the end of the infinitive.
Spanish speakers use both forms, although attaching it to the end is often considered more natural in spoken Spanish for some learners.
/lessons/direct-objects-3: Just like with infinitives, direct object pronouns can also be attached to present particles (-ando, -iendo).
Option 2: Attached to the end of the participle.
Notice that when you attach a pronoun to a present participle, you must add an accent mark to the original stressed vowel of the participle to maintain the correct stress.
